body_txt: In certain cultures and social groups, facial hair, hairstyles, and hair arrangements often conveyed social status, marital status, and even affiliations . For women , it is the Prim and Proper Bun and Power Hair . For men, it is the mustache. Curled and waxed mustaches are popular with high society because it is worn by aristocrats, businessmen, monarchs, and WASPs , often associated with those with authority, influence, and wealth. Mustaches, specifically curled and waxed mustaches, are a class marker worn by characters with high statuses, vast wealth, and ties to royal or aristocratic families. These styles of mustaches (i.e., English, Hungarian, and Imperial mustaches) evoke an image of sophistication, power, and respectability, signaling economic success and cultural or social standing. This trope associates mustaches with wealth and high status. Handlebar mustaches were among the most popular styles, easily recognized by their curled, waxed tips. This distinctive mustache was often paired with other facial hair styles, such as a goatee or a soul patch , creating a range of varied looks. The Verdi beard, which features a handlebar mustache, was popular among monarchs and royalty. The Van Dyke beard typically includes a mustache styled into a handlebar shape and was especially popular among European royalty during the 17th century. Waxed and curly mustaches were unpopular with the lower class, and these styles can show a class divide between the upper and lower class . In the 19th century, the working-class stache wasn't as eccentric or flamboyant. During this time, horseshoe, toothbrush, and walrus mustaches were popular because they only had to be brushed and trimmed as a requirement. World War II , however, put a swift end to the toothbrush mustache fashion trend . Upper-class characters are more likely to have mustaches because they want to uphold the traditional gentleman image and establish their status. It shows how idle they are , since working-class people were often clean-shaven so that gas masks could seal tightly and prevent the inhalation of hazardous materials. Therefore, a mustache implies that the character does not need to work or is not employed in such industries. The handlebar mustache was popular with businessmen, business tycoons, industrialists , the Gentleman Detective , and the Gentleman Thief . It implies that wearers want to project an image of professionalism, power, and social prominence. For criminals, the mustache is used to create a positive public image , suggesting they are respectable, legitimate businessmen and Neighbourhood-Friendly Gangsters through a debonair, charming style. The mustache serves as an aura of refinement and respectability, creating a mask of class that helps criminals gain trust, disarm suspicion, and blend into elite circles by appearing trustworthy and well-established. For the lower class, invoking this by adopting the handlebar mustache symbolizes an aspiration to move up the social ladder by projecting an image of respectability, refinement, or sophistication . They're tidying up so their mustaches look less bushy and unkempt , however, they will be called out by both classes for playing above their station by appearing pretentious or inauthentic in an attempt to look important or respectable. Unless they are the King of Thieves , a high-ranking criminal, or a member of the Thieves' Guild , dressing like an upper-class gentleman would be a non-verbal declaration of wealth and an open invitation to be robbed by thieves. Because of its association with the upper class, the handlebar mustache is primarily used for mockery, which would explain the origin behind the classic vaudeville villain's over-the-top handlebar mustache. In the 21st century, the handlebar mustache is seen as a bit more laughable and outdated for being so old-fashioned. It's exaggerated to imitate the stereotype of the wealthy, smug, and self-important person from the upper class. In the early 2000s, the Hipster movement adopted it because they saw it as vintage and old-fashioned. Not to be confused with Dastardly Whiplash , a cartoonish, archetypal villain with depthless schemes, sinister-looking clothing, and a meticulously trimmed handlebar mustache, but the tropes can overlap. Supertrope to the Dapper Pencil Mustache , a thin pencil mustache worn by suave and sophisticated men or men who are clean-cut and dapper. Not to be confused with the Horseshoe Mustache of Toughness , which is recognizable for being 2 vertical columns that reach the chin and resemble an upside-down "U" shape and thus has a more rough-and-tumble vibe. Can go with Manly Facial Hair . Goes with 'Stache of Duty , Quintessential British Gentleman , and The Von Trope Family . Contrast Porn Stache , which is meant for sleazy or lower-class folks.
